On Novig's peer-to-peer exchange, a limit order lets you set the price you're willing to accept — it waits in the market until someone matches it at that price or better, or it expires. A market order executes immediately against available counterparties at their current prices.

Use a limit order when you have a target price and can wait. Use a market order when you need to fill right away and accept the current market price.

Since Novig is commission-free, there's no fee difference between them — the choice is just about speed vs. price control.

Novig Support

The charts spinning on load suggests a rendering issue on our end. A few quick things to try:

- Hard refresh your browser (Cmd+Shift+R on Mac, Ctrl+Shift+R on Windows).
- Clear your browser cache for novig.com and reload.
- Try the web app in an incognito/private window.

If that doesn't fix it, let us know which browser version you're on and whether the issue is still happening. We'll loop in engineering to investigate.

Novig Support

Novig Support here — I see your $500 ACH deposit from 4 business days ago hasn't posted to your balance yet, past the 1-3 business day window.

I can't confirm whether it failed from here. I'm passing this to our payments team to look into it — it would help if you can reply with the last 4 digits of the bank account used and the approximate time you submitted the deposit.

Novig Support

Thanks for asking. On Novig's peer-to-peer exchange, your order gets matched against counterparties' orders in the book. Commonly, exchanges use price-time priority — best price first, then earliest timestamp — but I can't state that as Novig's exact rule.

If you have a specific order that didn't fill the way you expected, share the order ID and we can have our ops team look at what happened. For the full matching algorithm, a human can follow up with the details.
